title: Pre-Retirement Couple: Sequence-of-Returns Risk and Concentrated Stock
constraints: Sequence-of-returns risk in early retirement years
key_risks: No LTC insurance — $120-300K/year nursing home cost exposure
Sequence-of-returns risk if retiring into a down market
Medicare gap age 62-65 before retirement health coverage

planning_issues.description: 28% of investable portfolio in single employer stock; correlated with Robert's human capital
No LTC insurance; LTC costs in Minneapolis average $90,000–$150,000/year; age 58 is last reasonable window for affordable coverage
Sequence-of-returns risk: retiring at 62 with 30-year horizon requires inflation-adjusted withdrawal strategy
RMD planning starting age 73; Roth conversion window between retirement and age 73 is tax-efficient

key_recommendations: Systematic concentrated stock diversification over 3 years
Evaluate LTC insurance now — last affordable window at age 58
Model Social Security claiming strategies (62 vs 67 vs 70)
Roth conversion ladder in gap years between retirement and SS/RMD
Build 2-year cash/bond buffer against sequence-of-returns risk
follow_up_questions: What is the cost basis on the concentrated stock position? (NUA analysis)
Has Linda applied for Social Security earnings record review?
Does Robert's employer offer any pension or defined benefit component?
What are the LTC preferences — home care vs. facility?
outcome_summary: Divested 60% of concentrated position over 18 months. LTC insurance secured. SS claiming modeled to maximize lifetime benefits for both spouses.
key_lesson: Pre-retirement planning must address sequence-of-returns risk, concentrated positions, and LTC insurance simultaneously. The decade before retirement is the last cost-effective window for LTC coverage.
